title: Next Greater Element
description: For each element find the next greater element using stack

examples.explanation:
Initialize output = [-1, -1, -1, -1]

For i=0 (element 4):
  Check j=1: 5 > 4 → output[0] = 5, break

For i=1 (element 5):
  Check j=2: 2 ≤ 5 → continue
  Check j=3: 25 > 5 → output[1] = 25, break

For i=2 (element 2):
  Check j=3: 25 > 2 → output[2] = 25, break

For i=3 (element 25):
  No elements to check → output[3] remains -1

Final output: [5, 25, 25, -1]

Initialize output = [-1, -1, -1, -1]

For i=0 (element 13):
  Check j=1: 7 ≤ 13 → continue
  Check j=2: 6 ≤ 13 → continue
  Check j=3: 12 ≤ 13 → continue
  No greater element found → output[0] remains -1

For i=1 (element 7):
  Check j=2: 6 ≤ 7 → continue
  Check j=3: 12 > 7 → output[1] = 12, break

For i=2 (element 6):
  Check j=3: 12 > 6 → output[2] = 12, break

For i=3 (element 12):
  No elements to check → output[3] remains -1

Final output: [-1, 12, 12, -1]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each element, iterate through the remaining elements to find the first greater element. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Next Greater Element
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution finding next greater element for each element in array
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the next greater element for each element in an array is conceptually simple but computationally expensive. The intuition is straightforward: for each element, we look ahead in the array to find the first element that's larger than it. This works because we're scanning linearly from left to right, and the 'next' greater element is defined as the first element to the right that's larger. The algorithm starts by initializing an output array of the same size as the input, filled with -1 values (since -1 represents 'no greater element found'). For each element at index i, we iterate through all elements from i+1 to the end of the array. When we find the first element greater than arr[i], we store that value in output[i] and break the inner loop. If no greater element is found, output[i] remains -1. The time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n elements, we potentially scan through the remaining n-i elements. In the worst case (descending array), this becomes n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2 operations. The space complexity is O(1) excluding the output array, or O(n) if we count the output storage. This approach connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include: 1) Not breaking the inner loop after finding the first greater element (which would find the maximum, not the next greater), 2) Forgetting to handle the last element (which always has -1), 3) Using wrong comparison operators, and 4) Not initializing the output array properly. The real code pattern involves nested loops with careful index management and early termination.

examples.trace:
Initialize output = [-1, -1, -1, -1]

For i=0 (element 4):
  Check j=1: 5 > 4 → output[0] = 5, break

For i=1 (element 5):
  Check j=2: 2 ≤ 5 → continue
  Check j=3: 25 > 5 → output[1] = 25, break

For i=2 (element 2):
  Check j=3: 25 > 2 → output[2] = 25, break

For i=3 (element 25):
  No elements to check → output[3] remains -1

Final output: [5, 25, 25, -1]

Initialize output = [-1, -1, -1, -1]

For i=0 (element 13):
  Check j=1: 7 ≤ 13 → continue
  Check j=2: 6 ≤ 13 → continue
  Check j=3: 12 ≤ 13 → continue
  No greater element found → output[0] remains -1

For i=1 (element 7):
  Check j=2: 6 ≤ 7 → continue
  Check j=3: 12 > 7 → output[1] = 12, break

For i=2 (element 6):
  Check j=3: 12 > 6 → output[2] = 12, break

For i=3 (element 12):
  No elements to check → output[3] remains -1

Final output: [-1, 12, 12, -1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Next Greater for Single Element
ladders.desc: Find the next greater element for a specific element at given index
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the brute force approach - finding the next greater element for a single element at a specific index. The intuition is linear search: starting from the element immediately to the right, we scan forward until we either find a larger element or reach the end of the array. This works because 'next' implies immediate proximity in the forward direction, and we want the first occurrence, not necessarily the largest. The algorithm takes an array and a target index as input. We store the value at the target index as 'current'. Then we iterate through the array starting from target+1 to the end. For each element we encounter, we compare it with 'current'. If we find an element greater than 'current', we return that element immediately (since it's the first greater element). If we reach the end without finding a greater element, we return -1. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) in the worst case (when the target is at the beginning and no greater element exists), but typically O(k) where k is the distance to the next greater element. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the parent problem by being called for each element in the array. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting the search from the wrong index (should be target+1, not target), 2) Not handling the case where target is the last element, 3) Continuing to search after finding a greater element (which would return a later, not necessarily the next, greater element), and 4) Comparing with the wrong value. The real code pattern involves a simple for loop with an early return condition.

examples.trace:
Current element at index 1: 5
Start search from index 2:
  Index 2: 2 ≤ 5 → continue
  Index 3: 25 > 5 → found! Return 25

Current element at index 0: 13
Start search from index 1:
  Index 1: 7 ≤ 13 → continue
  Index 2: 6 ≤ 13 → continue
  Index 3: 12 ≤ 13 → continue
Reached end → no greater element found → return -1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Linear Forward Search Pattern
ladders.desc: The pattern of searching forward from a position to find first element meeting condition
ladders.explanation: This core logic represents the fundamental search pattern used in the brute force approach: starting from a given position, scan forward through the array to find the first element that satisfies a condition (in this case, being greater than the starting element). The intuition is sequential examination: we examine elements in their natural order until we find what we're looking for or exhaust all possibilities. This works because arrays are ordered sequences, and 'next' implies examining elements in their stored order. The algorithm involves maintaining a current position and a target value. We iterate through indices from start+1 to n-1. For each position, we compare the element with our target. If it satisfies the condition (greater than target), we return that element and position. If not, we continue. If we reach the end without success, we return a sentinel value (-1). The time complexity is O(n) in worst case, O(1) in best case (if the next element satisfies the condition). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the actual search mechanism. Common mistakes include: 1) Not updating the position correctly in the loop, 2) Using the wrong comparison condition, 3) Not handling the empty search range (when start is at the end), and 4) Confusing 'first greater' with 'maximum'. The real code pattern is a simple for loop with conditional check and early return.

examples.trace:
Start search from index 1:
  Index 1: 5 > 4 → condition satisfied! Return element 5 at index 1

Start search from index 1:
  Index 1: 7 ≤ 13 → continue
  Index 2: 6 ≤ 13 → continue
  Index 3: 12 ≤ 13 → continue
Reached end → no element satisfies condition → return -1, -1

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Nested Indices
ladders.desc: Navigating arrays using nested index variables to compare elements
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the fundamental skill of traversing an array using two index variables - one for the current element being examined, and another for searching forward from that position. The intuition is coordinate-based navigation: we use one index to 'point' to our reference element, and another to 'scan' forward through the remaining elements. This works because arrays provide random access via indices, allowing us to compare any two elements directly. The algorithm involves initializing an outer index i from 0 to n-1. For each i, we initialize an inner index j from i+1 to n-1. We compare arr[i] with arr[j] using the greater-than operator. If arr[j] > arr[i], we've found what we're looking for. If not, we increment j and continue. If j reaches n, we conclude no match exists for position i. The time complexity for this pattern is O(n²) when fully implemented, as each i may require scanning through potentially n-i elements. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the core logic by providing the index management framework. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, 2) Using the same variable for both indices, 3) Not resetting the inner index for each outer iteration, 4) Comparing arr[i] with arr[i] (self-comparison), and 5) Not handling the case where i is the last element (inner loop shouldn't run). The real code pattern involves nested for loops with careful boundary conditions.

examples.trace:
For i=1 (element 5):
  Initialize j=2
  Compare arr[1]=5 with arr[2]=2: 2 ≤ 5 → continue
  j=3
  Compare arr[1]=5 with arr[3]=25: 25 > 5 → found greater element

examples.output: Comparison results: index 1: 7 ≤ 13, index 2: 6 ≤ 13, index 3: 12 ≤ 13 (not found)
examples.trace:
For i=0 (element 13):
  Initialize j=1
  Compare arr[0]=13 with arr[1]=7: 7 ≤ 13 → continue
  j=2
  Compare arr[0]=13 with arr[2]=6: 6 ≤ 13 → continue
  j=3
  Compare arr[0]=13 with arr[3]=12: 12 ≤ 13 → continue
  j reaches end → no greater element found

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison in Arrays
ladders.desc: Comparing two array elements using their indices
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two elements in an array using their indices. The intuition is direct value comparison: given two positions in an array, we access the values at those positions and compare them using relational operators. This works because arrays store elements contiguously in memory, and we can access any element in constant time using its index. The algorithm requires: 1) An array, 2) Two valid indices i and j, 3) A comparison operator (in this case, greater than). We retrieve arr[i] and arr[j], then apply the comparison. The result is a boolean indicating whether the condition is satisfied. Time complexity is O(1) since array access and comparison are constant-time operations. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the building block by providing the fundamental comparison operation used in nested traversal. Common mistakes include: 1) Using indices outside array bounds, 2) Comparing indices instead of values (writing i > j instead of arr[i] > arr[j]), 3) Using the wrong comparison operator, 4) Not storing the values before comparison (which can be inefficient if values are complex objects), and 5) Assuming the array is sorted (it's not necessarily sorted). The real code pattern is a simple conditional statement comparing two array elements.

examples.trace:
Access arr[0] = 4
Access arr[1] = 5
Compare: 4 > 5 → false

Access arr[0] = 13
Access arr[3] = 12
Compare: 13 > 12 → true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in an array using zero-based indices
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the most basic operation: accessing elements in an array using indices. The intuition is positional addressing: an array is a contiguous block of memory where each element has a numerical position called an index. In most programming languages, arrays use zero-based indexing, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. This works because the computer can calculate the memory address of any element as: base_address + (index × element_size). The algorithm for accessing an element is straightforward: given an array and a valid index (between 0 and n-1 where n is the array length), we use the array[index] syntax to retrieve the value. Time complexity is O(1) since it's a direct memory access. Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation itself. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the mechanism to retrieve elements for comparison. Common mistakes include: 1) Using one-based indexing (thinking index 1 is the first element), 2) Accessing out-of-bounds indices (which causes errors), 3) Confusing the index with the value stored at that index, 4) Not checking if the index is valid before access, and 5) Assuming all arrays start at index 0 (some languages allow different lower bounds). The real code pattern is simply using square brackets with an index to access array elements.

examples.trace:
Array has 4 elements: indices 0, 1, 2, 3
Index 2 is valid (0 ≤ 2 < 4)
Access arr[2] → returns value 2

Array has 4 elements: indices 0, 1, 2, 3
Index 0 is valid (0 ≤ 0 < 4)
Access arr[0] → returns value 13

approaches.name: Stack Monotonic Decreasing O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to maintain a decreasing sequence of indices. For each element, pop from the stack while the current element is greater than the element at the stack's top index, assigning the next greater element. This efficiently processes elements in a single pass.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Next Greater Element Using Monotonic Decreasing Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding next greater element for each element in array using stack
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires finding, for each element in an array, the next element to its right that is strictly greater. If no such element exists, we output -1. The monotonic decreasing stack approach solves this efficiently in O(n) time and O(n) space. The intuition is that we process elements from left to right while maintaining a stack of indices where the corresponding elements are in decreasing order. When we encounter a current element that's greater than the element at the stack's top index, we've found the 'next greater element' for that stacked element. We pop from the stack and assign the current element as the answer for the popped index. This works because the stack maintains elements waiting for their next greater element, and we only pop when we find it. The algorithm: 1) Initialize result array with -1 and empty stack. 2) For each index i from 0 to n-1: While stack is not empty and nums[i] > nums[stack[-1]]: Pop top index j from stack, set result[j] = nums[i]. Push i onto stack. 3) Return result. Time complexity is O(n) because each element is pushed and popped at most once. Space is O(n) for stack and result. This connects to L1 as it uses the core popping logic repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling empty stack properly, confusing indices with values, and not initializing result with -1. Real code pattern: def nextGreaterElement(nums): n = len(nums); result = [-1]*n; stack = []; for i in range(n): while stack and nums[i] > nums[stack[-1]]: j = stack.pop(); result[j] = nums[i]; stack.append(i); return result.

examples.trace: Initialize: result = [-1, -1, -1, -1], stack = []. i=0, nums[0]=4: stack empty, push 0 → stack=[0]. i=1, nums[1]=5: while stack and 5>4: pop j=0, result[0]=5, stack=[]. Push 1 → stack=[1]. i=2, nums[2]=2: while stack and 2>5? false. Push 2 → stack=[1,2]. i=3, nums[3]=25: while stack and 25>2: pop j=2, result[2]=25, stack=[1]. while stack and 25>5: pop j=1, result[1]=25, stack=[]. Push 3 → stack=[3]. End loop. Return result=[5,25,25,-1].

Initialize: result = [-1, -1, -1, -1], stack = []. i=0, nums[0]=13: stack empty, push 0 → stack=[0]. i=1, nums[1]=7: while stack and 7>13? false. Push 1 → stack=[0,1]. i=2, nums[2]=6: while stack and 6>7? false. Push 2 → stack=[0,1,2]. i=3, nums[3]=12: while stack and 12>6: pop j=2, result[2]=12, stack=[0,1]. while stack and 12>7: pop j=1, result[1]=12, stack=[0]. while stack and 12>13? false. Push 3 → stack=[0,3]. End loop. Return result=[-1,12,12,-1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Popping Stack for Next Greater Element
ladders.desc: The while loop that pops from stack when current element is greater
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the algorithm: while stack is not empty and current element > element at stack's top index, pop from stack and assign current element as next greater for popped index. The intuition is that we maintain a stack of indices where elements are in decreasing order (monotonic decreasing). When we see a current element that breaks this decreasing pattern (i.e., it's greater than the top element), it serves as the 'next greater element' for all elements in the stack that are smaller than it. We pop them one by one because they've found their answer. This works because we process left to right, so the current element is the first greater element to the right for those popped indices. The algorithm: Given nums array, current index i, stack of indices, result array. While stack not empty and nums[i] > nums[stack[-1]]: j = stack.pop(); result[j] = nums[i]. Time complexity per element is amortized O(1) since each pop happens once. Space is O(n) for stack. This connects to L0 as it's repeated in the main loop. Common mistakes include not checking stack emptiness first (causing index error), using > instead of >= (for strictly greater), and not updating result correctly. Real code pattern: while stack and nums[i] > nums[stack[-1]]: j = stack.pop(); result[j] = nums[i].

examples.trace: Start: stack=[0] (index), nums[0]=10, nums[1]=20, result=[-1,-1,-1]. Condition: stack not empty? yes. nums[1]=20 > nums[0]=10? yes. Pop j=0 from stack → stack=[]. Set result[0]=20 → result=[20,-1,-1]. Condition: stack empty? yes, exit while. End: stack=[], result=[20,-1,-1].

Start: stack=[0,1], nums[0]=5, nums[1]=3, nums[2]=4, result=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. Condition: stack not empty? yes. nums[2]=4 > nums[1]=3? yes. Pop j=1 from stack → stack=[0]. Set result[1]=4 → result=[-1,4,-1,-1]. Condition: stack not empty? yes. nums[2]=4 > nums[0]=5? no. Exit while. End: stack=[0], result=[-1,4,-1,-1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Monotonic Decreasing Stack
ladders.desc: Using stack to keep indices of elements in decreasing order while processing
ladders.explanation: The core logic is to maintain a stack where the elements (via their indices) are in strictly decreasing order from bottom to top. As we process the array from left to right, we push the current index onto the stack only after popping all indices from the top where the corresponding elements are smaller than the current element. This ensures the stack remains monotonic decreasing. The intuition is that elements in the stack are 'waiting' for their next greater element. By keeping them in decreasing order, we guarantee that when we find a current element greater than the top, it's also greater than all elements below (since they're larger or equal), but we only pop the top repeatedly. This structure allows efficient O(n) processing. The algorithm: For each index i, while stack not empty and nums[i] > nums[stack[-1]], pop from stack. Then push i onto stack. Time per element is O(1) amortized. Space O(n). This connects to L1 as it sets up the stack for popping. Common mistakes include not popping enough (breaking monotonic property) or popping when equal (should use > not >= for strictly greater). Real code pattern: for i in range(n): while stack and nums[i] > nums[stack[-1]]: stack.pop(); stack.append(i).

examples.trace: Start stack=[]. i=0, nums[0]=3: stack empty, push 0 → stack=[0]. i=1, nums[1]=1: while stack and 1>3? false. Push 1 → stack=[0,1] (values 3,1 decreasing). i=2, nums[2]=4: while stack and 4>1: pop top → stack=[0]. while stack and 4>3: pop top → stack=[]. Push 2 → stack=[2]. i=3, nums[3]=2: while stack and 2>4? false. Push 3 → stack=[2,3] (values 4,2 decreasing). End: stack=[2,3].

Start stack=[]. i=0, nums[0]=5: push 0 → stack=[0]. i=1, nums[1]=4: while stack and 4>5? false. Push 1 → stack=[0,1]. i=2, nums[2]=3: while stack and 3>4? false. Push 2 → stack=[0,1,2]. i=3, nums[3]=2: while stack and 2>3? false. Push 3 → stack=[0,1,2,3]. i=4, nums[4]=1: while stack and 1>2? false. Push 4 → stack=[0,1,2,3,4]. All values decreasing: 5,4,3,2,1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing and Updating Based on Array Values
ladders.desc: Comparing current element with previous elements using indices
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves comparing the current element in an array with elements at specific indices stored in a data structure (here, stack). We use indices to access array values and make decisions based on comparisons. The intuition is that instead of storing values directly, we store indices to preserve order and enable updates to a result array. When we compare nums[i] > nums[stack[-1]], we're checking if the current value is greater than the value at the last stored index. If true, we update the result for that index. This allows us to track 'next greater' relationships efficiently. The algorithm: Access current value nums[i]. Access top index from stack: top_idx = stack[-1]. Compare nums[i] with nums[top_idx]. If greater, update result[top_idx] = nums[i]. Time O(1) per comparison. Space O(1) extra. This connects to L2 as it's the comparison step in the while loop. Common mistakes include comparing indices instead of values, or accessing stack[-1] when stack is empty. Real code pattern: if stack: top_idx = stack[-1]; if nums[i] > nums[top_idx]: result[top_idx] = nums[i].

examples.output: Compare: nums[2]=10 > nums[1]=6? yes, update result[1]=10
examples.trace: Given: nums=[8,6,10], i=2 (current index), stack=[0,1] (indices), result=[-1,-1,-1]. Step 1: Check stack not empty? yes. Step 2: Get top index: top_idx = stack[-1] = 1. Step 3: Get values: current_val = nums[2] = 10, top_val = nums[1] = 6. Step 4: Compare: 10 > 6? yes. Step 5: Update result for top_idx: result[1] = 10. Result becomes [-1,10,-1]. Note: This is a single comparison; in full algorithm, we'd pop and continue.

examples.output: Compare: nums[1]=5 > nums[0]=5? no, no update
examples.trace: Given: nums=[5,5,3], i=1, stack=[0], result=[-1,-1,-1]. Step 1: Stack not empty? yes. Step 2: top_idx = stack[-1] = 0. Step 3: current_val = nums[1] = 5, top_val = nums[0] = 5. Step 4: Compare: 5 > 5? no (strictly greater required). Step 5: No update. Result remains [-1,-1,-1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with Indices
ladders.desc: Fundamental stack operations using indices as elements
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a stack data structure where we push and pop indices (integers representing positions in an array). Pushing adds an index to the top of the stack, and popping removes the top index. The intuition is that a stack follows LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) order, which is ideal for processing elements in reverse order of encounter. By storing indices instead of values, we can access both the value (via array) and update results for that index. The algorithm: Initialize empty stack list. Push: stack.append(index). Pop: index = stack.pop(). Check top without popping: top_index = stack[-1] if stack else None. Time O(1) per operation. Space O(n) for stack storage. This connects to L3 as it provides the mechanism to store and retrieve indices for comparison. Common mistakes include popping from empty stack (use check), confusing push/pop order, or using wrong index values. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(i); j = stack.pop(); if stack: top = stack[-1].

examples.input: Start stack=[], push 0, push 1, pop, push 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial stack = []. Step 2: Push index 0 → stack.append(0) → stack=[0]. Step 3: Push index 1 → stack.append(1) → stack=[0,1]. Step 4: Pop → j = stack.pop() removes top element 1 → stack=[0], j=1. Step 5: Push index 2 → stack.append(2) → stack=[0,2]. Final stack has indices 0 and 2.

examples.input: Start stack=[5,3], pop, push 7, pop, push 9
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial stack = [5,3] (indices). Step 2: Pop → j = stack.pop() removes 3 → stack=[5], j=3. Step 3: Push index 7 → stack.append(7) → stack=[5,7]. Step 4: Pop → j = stack.pop() removes 7 → stack=[5], j=7. Step 5: Push index 9 → stack.append(9) → stack=[5,9]. Final stack has indices 5 and 9.

testCases.input: stack=[], push 6, push 7, push 8, pop, pop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Value Access
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in an array using indices (positions)
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: an array is a collection of elements where each element has an index (position) starting from 0. We can access the value at a specific index using array[index]. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. The intuition is that indices allow us to refer to elements without knowing their values directly, enabling operations like comparison or updates based on position. This is fundamental because in the stack approach, we store indices to later access values and update results at those indices. The algorithm: Given array arr and index i, access value = arr[i]. Update value: arr[i] = new_value. Time O(1). Space O(1). This connects to L4 as we use indices in stack operations to then access array values. Common mistakes include using out-of-bounds indices (e.g., arr[3] for length 3 array), confusing index with value, or off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: arr = [5, 3, 8]; x = arr[1]  # x=3; arr[0] = 10  # arr becomes [10,3,8].

examples.trace: Given array arr = [4, 7, 1]. Index 1 refers to the second element (since indices start at 0). Access arr[1]: look at position 1 in the array, which holds value 7. So output is 7.
Given array arr = [9, 2, 5]. We want to update the element at index 0. Current arr[0] = 9. Set arr[0] = 6. This changes the first element from 9 to 6. The array is now [6, 2, 5].
